mnemonics: PASS BY WHAT? (P: Pass by Pointer, R: Pass by Reference, V: Pass by Value)
CRASH Test: Const References Avoid Slice & Help performance
keyPoints: Objects can be passed to functions by value (creates a copy), by reference (uses the original object), or by pointer (passes the memory address).
Passing by const reference is highly efficient for large objects as it avoids the overhead of copying while preventing the function from modifying the original object.
Passing by value (copy) leads to object slicing when a derived class object is passed to a function expecting a base class object; this slices off the derived class's additional members.
commonMistakes: Modifying the original object unintentionally by passing it by non-const reference when a copy was intended.
Forgetting the ampersand (&) for a reference parameter, which defaults to pass-by-value and creates an expensive copy.
Causing object slicing by passing a derived class object by value to a function that accepts a base class parameter, losing the derived class's specific attributes.
